slateTag: HERBERT TRIPLE, RAIDERS TRIPLE, ATTACK DALLAS DEFENSE

analysis.stackSummary:
This roster wins with two concentrated clusters and one simple read. The primary cluster is the Chargers passing tree through Justin Herbert, Quentin Johnston, and Keenan Allen. Los Angeles lands 34 points in Dallas, and the build captures multiple routes to touchdowns without forcing a bring back. Dallas had been a target all season, so the click path is straightforward: attack the defense first, then decide which Chargers can access ceiling.

The second cluster is the Raiders triple at low combined ownership. The common story says RB plus defense must score together, yet this result shows outcome dependence can mislead. Las Vegas defense posts one point, and the lineup survives because Ashton Jeanty carries the stack on his own at two percent ownership. Brock Bowers adds a modest 14.3, and tight end volatility makes that total acceptable when other positions carry slate breaking scores.

The rest of the roster is clean allocation. James Cook III and Chase Brown are independent eruption scores from separate games, so the build does not require a single game environment to be perfect. Chris Olave supplies a standalone wide receiver ceiling from a different matchup, keeping raw points high while ownership stays contained.
analysis.uniquenessNotes:
The differentiator is structure, not a single hidden play. Herbert triple creates a three player bet on one offensive outcome, yet total roster ownership stays low because the secondary stack is Raiders heavy at small percentages and the one offs are spread across four additional games. Many lineups chase perfect correlation. This roster takes correlation where it matters and accepts imperfect secondary details.

Raiders defense failing creates a useful lesson: bad results in one slot do not erase a lineup when the rest of the roster carries multiple independent ceiling lanes. Jeanty at two percent supplies separation, and the build avoids over investing in one game script by pairing him with Cook, Brown, and Olave from three other game environments.

stackMeta.primaryLever: Herbert triple with no bring back in a game where Dallas defense invited concentrated exposure
stackMeta.secondaryLever: Raiders triple anchored by Jeanty ceiling while the defense score became a non factor

lineupAnalysis.macroStory: A two cluster roster where the Chargers passing outcome supplies the primary scoring engine and the Raiders game supplies leverage through Jeanty. Independent ceiling scores across Cook, Brown, and Olave remove reliance on any single additional game environment.

lineupItemAnalysis.microStory: Herbert provides the main scoring lane. At seven percent ownership, 33.2 points is the type of output that lets a concentrated stack compete with more common roster builds.
Johnston gives the triple its leverage. One score plus 100 yard bonus performance at 1.5 percent ownership creates separation without needing a Dallas return.

Jeanty is the reason the Raiders cluster stays profitable even with defense scoring failure. His two percent ownership attaches a ceiling outcome to a roster already carrying a high scoring quarterback stack.
Raiders defense scores one point against C.J. Stroud, yet the lineup remains live because the roster has multiple higher priority ceiling paths. This slot becomes a tolerated miss, not a sink.
